question: Define the norm (length) of a vector in an inner product space. Using the definition of the inner product, prove that for any scalar c and vector v, ||c v|| = |c| * ||v||.
expectedPoints: Definition of the norm: ||v|| = sqrt(<v, v>)
Apply the norm to the vector c v: ||c v|| = sqrt(<c v, c v>)
Use the linearity properties of the inner product: <c v, c v> = c * conjugate(c) * <v, v> = |c|^2 <v, v>
Take the square root: sqrt(|c|^2 <v, v>) = |c| * sqrt(<v, v>) = |c| * ||v||

question: State the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality. Explain its significance in defining the angle between two vectors in a real inner product space and how it guarantees the geometric interpretation of the inner product is well-defined.
expectedPoints: Explain its role in defining angle: cos(θ) = <u, v> / (||u|| ||v||). The inequality ensures |cos(θ)| ≤ 1.
Discuss how this guarantees the quotient is always a valid number between -1 and 1, making the angle definition meaningful.

question: Describe the Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization Process. Walk through the steps to transform a given basis {v1, v2, v3} for a subspace into an orthogonal basis {u1, u2, u3}. Provide the general formulas for u1, u2, and u3, and explain the purpose of subtracting the projection in each step.
expectedPoints: Purpose: To convert a linearly independent set into an orthogonal (or orthonormal) set spanning the same subspace.
Explanation: Subtracting the projection removes the component of the new vector that lies in the span of the previously orthogonalized vectors, ensuring the result is orthogonal to all of them.
